{"messages":[{"content":"You are a disciplined wiki maintainer following the Karpathy LLM Wiki pattern.
Output follows Open Knowledge Format (OKF) v0.1 conventions.

## Wiki Schema
# Wiki Schema & Conventions

This wiki follows Open Knowledge Format (OKF) v0.1 conventions.
See https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/knowledge-catalog/blob/main/okf/SPEC.md

## Directory Structure

```
raw/                  Immutable source files (not indexed)
wiki/                 Wiki pages (free-form, no prescribed subdirectory layout)
  index.md            Content catalog (maintained by ingest agent)
  log.md              Chronological record (maintained by ingest agent)
WIKI_SCHEMA.md        This file
.gmd/config.cue       GMD configuration
```

## Naming Conventions

- Use kebab-case for page filenames: `transformer-architecture.md`
- Entity pages: singular noun (`kubernetes.md`, `attention-mechanism.md`)
- Concept pages: descriptive phrase (`scaled-dot-product-attention.md`)
- Comparison pages: `a-vs-b.md` format
- Source pages: `YYYY-MM-DD-short-title.md` format
- Synthesis pages: `YYYY-MM-DD-question-slug.md` format

## Page Format

Every wiki page must:
1. Start with YAML frontmatter (between `---` delimiters)
2. Have a single H1 heading matching the page title
3. Use standard markdown links for cross-references: `[Page Name](/path/to/page.md)`
4. End with a "See Also" section where applicable

## Frontmatter Schema

```yaml
---
type: concept          # REQUIRED by OKF: free-form string
title: Page Title      # OKF recommended: display title
description: Summary   # OKF recommended: auto-generated by summarizer
resource: https://...  # OKF recommended: canonical URI
tags: [tag1, tag2]     # OKF recommended: lowercase, kebab-case
timestamp: 2026-06-13T14:30:00Z  # OKF recommended: ISO 8601, set by tool on write
# GMD extensions:
status: draft          # Free-form: draft, reviewed, needs-update
sources: [page.md]     # Pages this content derives from
---
```

## The Index File (index.md)

The index is the wiki's table of contents. It is maintained by the ingest agent
and should not be manually edited. Format:

```markdown
# Wiki Index

## Entities
* [Entity Name](/wiki/entities/entity-name.md) — One-line description
* ...

## Concepts
* [Concept Name](/wiki/concepts/concept-name.md) — One-line description
* ...

## Comparisons
* [A vs B](/wiki/comparisons/a-vs-b.md) — One-line description
* ...

## Sources
* [Source Title](/wiki/sources/YYYY-MM-DD-title-slug.md) — Summary and date ingested
* ...

## Last Updated
YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM
```

## The Log File (log.md)

The log records every ingest operation chronologically. It is maintained by the
ingest agent and should not be manually edited. Format:

```markdown
# Wiki Log

## [2026-05-31 14:30] ingest | Source Title
- Created: entities/foo.md, concepts/bar.md
- Updated: entities/baz.md
- Flagged contradiction: claim X vs existing page Y

## [2026-05-30 10:15] query-save | "question text"
- Saved: wiki/synthesis/2026-05-30-question-slug.md
```

## Status Lifecycle

```
draft → reviewed → needs-update → reviewed → ...
```

- draft: Initial creation, needs review
- reviewed: Verified for accuracy
- needs-update: New information available, needs revision


## Existing Wiki Pages

","role":"system"},{"content":"# Go Channels

Channels are a fundamental concurrency primitive in Go. They provide a way for goroutines to communicate with each other and synchronize their execution.

## Buffered vs Unbuffered

Unbuffered channels block the sender until a receiver is ready. Buffered channels allow sending up to the buffer capacity before blocking.

## Select Statement

The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations simultaneously. It picks one that is ready randomly.

## Use Cases

Channels are commonly used for:
- Pipeline patterns between goroutines
- Fan-out / fan-in patterns
- Timeout and cancellation signals
- Mutual exclusion via a single goroutine

## Best Practices

Always close channels when done sending. Use range to receive values until the channel is closed. Avoid using channels for pure mutual exclusion — use sync.Mutex instead.","role":"user"}],"model":"Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8"}

They provide a way for goroutines to communicate with each other and synchronize their execution.\n\n## Key Characteristics\n- Enable safe communication between concurrent goroutines\n- Provide built-in synchronization mechanisms\n- Support multiple usage patterns (pipelines, fan-out/fan-in, timeouts)\n\n## Common Use Cases\n- Pipeline patterns between goroutines\n- Fan-out / fan-in patterns for parallel processing\n- Timeout and cancellation signals\n- Coordination and synchronization (prefer `sync.Mutex` for pure mutual exclusion)\n\nSee Also: [Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels](/wiki/concepts/buffered-vs-unbuffered-channels.md), [Select Statement](/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md), [Channels vs Mutex](/wiki/comparisons/channels-vs-mutex.md)\n\n---\ntype: entity\ntitle: Go Channels\ntags: [go, concurrency, channels, synchronization]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/concepts/buffered-vs-unbuffered-channels.md\", \"/wiki/concepts/select-statement.md\", \"/wiki/comparisons/channels-vs-mutex.md\"]\nclaims: [\"Channels are a fundamental concurrency primitive in Go.\", \"Channels enable goroutine communication and synchronization.\"]\n    ",

      "content": "---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels\ntags: [go, channels, concurrency, buffering, synchronization]\nstatus: draft\n---\n# Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels\n\nChannels in Go can operate in two modes based on whether they allocate internal storage for messages.\n\n## Unbuffered Channels\n- Block the sender until a receiver is ready\n- Enforce synchronous communication\n- Guarantee that send and receive operations happen simultaneously\n\n## Buffered Channels\n- Allow sending up to the buffer capacity before blocking\n- Enable asynchronous communication within the capacity limit\n- Useful for decoupling producers and consumers or smoothing out bursty workloads\n\nSee Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md)\n\n---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels\ntags: [go, channels, concurrency, buffering, synchronization]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/entities/go-channels.md\"]\nclaims: [\"Unbuffered channels block the sender until a receiver is ready.\", \"Buffered channels allow sending up to the buffer capacity before blocking.\"]\n    ",

      "content": "---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Select Statement\ntags: [go, select, channels, concurrency, control-flow]\nstatus: draft\n---\n# Select Statement\n\nThe `select` statement is a control flow construct that allows a goroutine to wait on multiple channel operations.\n\n## Behavior\n- Waits on multiple channel send/receive operations simultaneously\n- Randomly selects one ready operation when multiple are available\n- Blocks indefinitely if no operations are ready (unless a `default` case is provided)\n\n## Common Patterns\n- Multiplexing channel inputs\n- Implementing timeouts and cancellations\n- Event loop and signal handling\n\nSee Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md)\n\n---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Select Statement\ntags: [go, select, channels, concurrency, control-flow]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/entities/go-channels.md\"]\nclaims: [\"The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations simultaneously.\", \"It picks one that is ready randomly.\"]\n    ",

      "content": "---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Pipeline Pattern\ntags: [go, pipeline, concurrency, channels, design-pattern]\nstatus: draft\n---\n# Pipeline Pattern\n\nA pipeline pattern structures data processing as a series of stages connected by channels, where each stage consists of one or more goroutines.\n\n## Structure\n- **Generator**: Produces initial data values\n- **Processor**: Transforms, filters, or enriches data\n- **Collector**: Aggregates or prints final results\n\n## Benefits\n- Encourages modularity and single-responsibility stages\n- Naturally supports concurrency and backpressure management\n- Data flows asynchronously between stages\n\nSee Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md), [Fan-out/Fan-in Pattern](/wiki/concepts/fan-out-fan-in-pattern.md)\n\n---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Pipeline Pattern\ntags: [go, pipeline, concurrency, channels, design-pattern]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/entities/go-channels.md\", \"/wiki/concepts/fan-out-fan-in-pattern.md\"]\nclaims: [\"Channels are commonly used for pipeline patterns between goroutines.\"]\n    ",

      "content": "---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Fan-out/Fan-in Pattern\ntags: [go, fan-out, fan-in, concurrency, channels, parallelism]\nstatus: draft\n---\n# Fan-out/Fan-in Pattern\n\nThis concurrency pattern distributes work across multiple goroutines and aggregates their results into a unified stream.\n\n## Mechanism\n- **Fan-out**: Multiple consumer goroutines read from a single input channel\n- **Fan-in**: Results from multiple producers are merged into a single output channel\n\n## Benefits\n- Maximizes CPU utilization through parallel execution\n- Dynamically scalable based on workload and resource availability\n- Simplifies parallel task distribution without manual thread management\n\nSee Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md), [Pipeline Pattern](/wiki/concepts/pipeline-pattern.md)\n\n---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Fan-out/Fan-in Pattern\ntags: [go, fan-out, fan-in, concurrency, channels, parallelism]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/entities/go-channels.md\", \"/wiki/concepts/pipeline-pattern.md\"]\nclaims: [\"Channels support fan-out/fan-in patterns for distributing and aggregating work.\"]\n    ",

      "content": "---\ntype: comparison\ntitle: Channels vs Mutex\ntags: [go, channels, mutex, concurrency, synchronization, comparison]\nstatus: draft\n---\n# Channels vs Mutex\n\nBoth channels and `sync.Mutex` are concurrency primitives in Go, but they solve different problems and follow different idioms.\n\n## Channels\n- **Primary Purpose**: Communication and coordination between goroutines\n- **Mechanism**: Passes typed data safely across concurrent tasks\n- **Best Suited For**: Pipelines, producer-consumer queues, event signaling, timeouts\n\n## sync.Mutex\n- **Primary Purpose**: Mutual exclusion to protect shared state\n- **Mechanism**: Acquires a lock before entering a critical section, releasing it afterward\n- **Best Suited For**: Protecting shared variables, preventing race conditions, simple critical sections\n\n## Guidelines\n- Avoid using channels for pure mutual exclusion; prefer `sync.Mutex` for better performance and clarity\n- Use channels when goroutines need to exchange data or coordinate execution flow\n- Combine both strategically: channels for routing/messaging, mutexes for internal shared state protection\n\nSee Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md)\n\n---\ntype: comparison\ntitle: Channels vs Mutex\ntags: [go, channels, mutex, concurrency, synchronization, comparison]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/entities/go-channels.md\"]\nclaims: [\"Avoid using channels for pure mutual exclusion — use sync.Mutex instead.\"]\n    ",

   - content: "# Select Statement\n\nThe `select` statement is a control flow construct in Go that enables a goroutine to wait on multiple channel operations.\n\n## Behavior\n- Waits on multiple channel send/receive operations simultaneously\n- Randomly selects one ready operation when multiple are available\n- Blocks if no operations are ready (unless a `default` case is provided)\n\n## Use Cases\n- Multiplexing channel operations\n- Implementing timeouts and cancellations\n- Event handling loops\n\nSee Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md)\n\n---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Select Statement\nconcept_kind: concept\ntags: [go, select, channels, concurrency, control-flow]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/entities/go-channels.md\"]\nclaims: [\"The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations simultaneously.\", \"It picks one that is ready randomly.\"]"

   **Concept: Pipeline Pattern**
   - page: concepts/pipeline-pattern.md
   - content: "# Pipeline Pattern\n\nA pipeline pattern processes data through a series of stages, where each stage consists of goroutines connected by channels.\n\n## Structure\n- **Generator**: Produces initial data\n- **Processor**: Transforms or filters data\n- **Collector**: Gathers final results\n\n## Benefits\n- Encourages modularity and reusability\n- Naturally supports concurrency and parallelism\n- Data flows asynchronously through stages\n\nSee Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md), [Fan-out/Fan-in Pattern](/wiki/concepts/fan-out-fan-in-pattern.md)\n\n---\ntype: concept\ntitle: Pipeline Pattern\nconcept_kind: concept\ntags: [go, pipeline, concurrency, channels, design-pattern]\nstatus: draft\nlinks_to: [\"/wiki/entities/go-channels.md\", \"/wiki/concepts/fan-out-fan-in-pattern.md\"]\nclaims: [\"Channels are commonly used for pipeline patterns between goroutines.\"]"

   - page: concepts/fan-out-fan-in-pattern.md

# Buffered vs Unbuffered Channels

Channels in Go can operate in two modes based on whether they allocate internal storage for messages.

## Unbuffered Channels
- Block the sender until a receiver is ready
- Enforce synchronous communication
- Guarantee that send and receive operations happen simultaneously

## Buffered Channels
- Allow sending up to the buffer capacity before blocking
- Enable asynchronous communication within the capacity limit
- Useful for decoupling producers and consumers or smoothing out bursty workloads

See Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md)

# Select Statement

The `select` statement is a control flow construct that allows a goroutine to wait on multiple channel operations.

## Behavior
- Waits on multiple channel send/receive operations simultaneously
- Randomly selects one ready operation when multiple are available

## Common Patterns

# Pipeline Pattern

A pipeline pattern structures data processing as a series of stages connected by channels, where each stage consists of one or more goroutines.

## Structure
- **Generator**: Produces initial data values
- **Processor**: Transforms, filters, or enriches data
- **Collector**: Aggregates or prints final results

## Benefits
- Encourages modularity and single-responsibility stages
- Naturally supports concurrency and backpressure management
- Data flows asynchronously between stages

See Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md), [Fan-out/Fan-in Pattern](/wiki/concepts/fan-out-fan-in-pattern.md)

# Fan-out/Fan-in Pattern

This concurrency pattern distributes work across multiple goroutines and aggregates their results into a unified stream.

## Mechanism
- **Fan-out**: Multiple consumer goroutines read from a single input channel
- **Fan-in**: Results from multiple producers are merged into a single output channel

## Benefits
- Maximizes CPU utilization through parallel execution
- Dynamically scalable based on workload and resource availability
- Simplifies parallel task distribution without manual thread management

See Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md), [Pipeline Pattern](/wiki/concepts/pipeline-pattern.md)

tags: [go, channels, mutex, concurrency, synchronization, comparison]

---
# Channels vs Mutex

Both channels and `sync.Mutex` are concurrency primitives in Go, but they solve different problems and follow different idioms.

## Channels
- **Primary Purpose**: Communication and coordination between goroutines
- **Mechanism**: Passes typed data safely across concurrent tasks
- **Best Suited For**: Pipelines, producer-consumer queues, event signaling, timeouts

## sync.Mutex
- **Primary Purpose**: Mutual exclusion to protect shared state
- **Mechanism**: Acquires a lock before entering a critical section, releasing it afterward
- **Best Suited For**: Protecting shared variables, preventing race conditions, simple critical sections

## Guidelines
- Avoid using channels for pure mutual exclusion; prefer `sync.Mutex` for better performance and clarity
- Use channels when goroutines need to exchange data or coordinate execution flow
- Combine both strategically: channels for routing/messaging, mutexes for internal shared state protection

See Also: [Go Channels](/wiki/entities/go-channels.md)
